## search_stability — 200 row(s)
| metal_27 | Ce^[3+] | ligand_10162 | Hydrogen fluoride (Hydroflu… | HL | F | 6 | 2 | 25 | 0~1 | solid | 4 |

paragraphs.text:
- Fluoride stands out for hard‑acid 3+ cations. In the retrieved subset, fluoride complexes dominate the stability data; trivalent hard ions (including Ce(III) among the lanthanides, plus Am(III)/Cm(III)/Bk(III)/Cf(III) actinides) show the strongest F− binding, well above divalent transitions and alkaline earths. A qualitative ranking from this set is: 3+ hard ions (Al3+, f‑element 3+) >> Be2+ >> Co2+/Cd2+ ≳ Ca2+/Ba2+ >> Ag+. This reflects charge density control of F− complexation. Note: in this subset, only Ce(III) appears for Ln(III), so a numeric La→Lu trend can’t be extracted here.
- Macrocycles in this slice aren’t Ln-focused. Polyoxa/diaza macrocycles (mixed O,N donors) show modest stability mainly with Ag+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, and essentially no lanthanide entries. This contrasts with the strong F− binding above and underscores why hard O‑donor ligands (e.g., fluoride, phosphonates) are more effective for targeting REEs.

paragraphs.text: If you’d like numeric constants, I can widen the search (e.g., include “oxalato/oxalic”, “malonato”, broader ionic strengths, or relax the strict 25 °C filter) to assemble a La→Lu set and illustrate the lanthanide‑contraction trend directly. Or we can zoom in on a specific pair (e.g., Nd–oxalate or Y–fluoride) and pull best‑conditioned log β values with citations.
